title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone

notes.text: Call to Adventure: Hogwarts letters begin arriving, breaking the Dursleys' control over Harry's life.
Yearns for belonging and identity but still sees himself as powerless.
Refusal of the Call: The Dursleys isolate Harry and destroy letters, trying to force him back into normalcy.
The refusal comes from external oppression, not from Harry's lack of desire.
Supernatural Aid: Hagrid arrives, tells Harry the truth, and opens the door to the wizarding world.
Bridge figure who offers warmth, truth, and practical guidance.
Crossing the First Threshold: Platform 9 3/4 and the Hogwarts Express mark Harry's full entry into a new reality.
Sorting ceremony commits him to Gryffindor, symbolically choosing courage over status.
Belly of the Whale: Hogwarts becomes total immersion - new rules, houses, rivalries, and hidden dangers.
Draco conflict and Snape suspicion establish that this world is not merely wondrous but contested.
Road of Trials: Troll attack, Quidditch sabotage, and forbidden-corridor mystery test Harry's courage and judgment.
Friendship forms through shared risk and complementary skills.
Meeting with the Goddess: Harry experiences deep emotional longing in the Mirror of Erised and confronts what he truly wants.
Desire for family becomes the emotional center that later guides his moral choices.
Woman as Temptress: The temptation is not romantic but psychological - to live in comforting illusion (Mirror) rather than reality.
Dumbledore's warning reframes temptation as fixation on fantasy.
Atonement with the Father: Harry confronts authority, legacy, and fear through Dumbledore's guidance and the looming shadow of Voldemort.
Father-like moral authority who teaches Harry that choices matter more than ability.
Apotheosis: Harry realizes he must actively protect the Stone; he is no longer a passive student waiting for adults.
Maturity begins when responsibility overrides fear.
The Ultimate Boon: At the Mirror in the chamber, Harry obtains the Stone because he seeks to protect it, not possess it.
This is the key ideological beat: selfless intent unlocks power.
Refusal of the Return: Harry is drawn toward the intensity of the magical world and the heroic confrontation rather than a simple child life.
The story hints he now belongs to a larger war, not just a school-year mystery.
The Magic Flight: After facing Quirrell/Voldemort, Harry is rescued back from danger and loss of consciousness.
Embodies hunger for power without love - Harry's ethical opposite.
Rescue from Without: Dumbledore and the school's protection framework preserve Harry after the confrontation.
The hero still needs community; individual courage works within a network of care.
Crossing of the Return Threshold: House Cup and term ending reintegrate Harry into ordinary school life with a transformed status.
Public recognition of Gryffindor reframes Harry from outsider to valued member of a house and community.
Master of Two Worlds: Harry can now move between Muggle and magical identities with awareness of who he is in both.
No longer defined by the cupboard or by fame alone - he has chosen his values.
Freedom to Live: Harry returns for summer changed, carrying friendship, purpose, and a future that is no longer dictated by the Dursleys.
Love, loyalty, and moral choice provide the freedom that power alone cannot.
